Text:
Most people think Nosht is a notes app. It's a bit more than that.

49 structured templates. 68 patient leaflets. 14 chairside tools — LA max-dose, NHS bands, perio staging, CPD logging, finance.

The whole UK dental day in one place, instead of five browser tabs and a drawer of printouts.

What would you want it to do next?
More than a notes app — 49 templates, 68 leaflets and 14 chairside tools in one place.

Watch a clinical note build itself. Type the shorthand you already think in; Nosht structures the fields; you review and confirm every one. A defensible note in under a minute. #dentistry #dentist #ukdentist #dentaltok #dentalnurse

An indemnity reviewer can tell a defensible note from a risky one in about five seconds.

Here's what they scan for:
1. Screening recorded — BPE, soft tissues, dated.
2. Clinical reasoning, not just findings.
3. Options + consent — alternatives, risks, agreement.
4. Advice + patient response.
5. Who, when, contemporaneous.

When a complaint lands months later, the contemporaneous record is the defence — regardless of how good the care actually was.

Which of the five does your current note most often miss?
A routine note in under a minute: type the shorthand you think in, Nosht structures the fields, you confirm each one.
Everyone thinks Nosht is just a notes app. It's a bit more — 49 templates, 68 leaflets, 14 chairside tools. The whole dental day in one place. #dentistry #dentist #ukdentist #dentaltok #dentalnurse

"AI dental notes" hides two very different things.

AI-generated: the tool writes the note for you; you skim and accept.

AI-assisted: you write it in shorthand, the tool structures it, and you review and sign every field.

For your GDC registration the difference is everything — one record is genuinely yours and contemporaneous, the other isn't. If you're choosing a tool this year, ask which one it actually is.
AI-assisted and AI-generated aren't the same thing — and only one is something you'd sign your name to.

Unpopular opinion: free-text is where defensibility goes to die. Structured wins on what every audit checks — consistency, completeness, compliance. #dentistry #dentist #ukdentist #dentaltok #dentalnurse

You trained for years to do dentistry. Not data entry.

A note isn't slow because anyone types slowly — it's slow because you reformat what you already know into a defensible paragraph, every single time.

Structured templates flip that: jot the shorthand the way you'd say it, review the result, paste it into your PMS. The judgement stays yours; the retyping disappears.

Which admin task would you bin first?
An indemnity reviewer reads a note in about five seconds. Screening, reasoning, options, consent, signature — in every note, or not.
"NAD." "…NAD." "NAD again." The most-typed phrase in dentistry. The routine bits fill in a tap — you still review the judgement calls. #dentistry #dentist #ukdentist #dentaltok #dentalnurse

A routine note shouldn't take longer than the filling did.

With Nosht you type the shorthand you already think in — MOD comp A2, RD, bite checked — and it structures the fields. You review and confirm each one, then paste straight into your PMS.

Under a minute for a routine note, and you've signed off every word.

Unpopular, but the audits agree: structured notes win on the three things that matter — consistency, completeness, compliance.

Where do your notes sit?
On a busy NHS list, the same structure on every patient is your best defence.

Consent isn't a signature. It's a conversation.

Montgomery made it clear: material risks, reasonable alternatives, and the patient's agreement — documented at the time, in their terms.
